metadata.title: How the Internet Routes Without a Map
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = distributed convergence / correct shortest path; CRIMSON = naive expectation of global map
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no convergence proof, no negative-cost loop, no BGP/OSPF engineering details. Wonder register.

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: An email leaves Stanford bound for Cornell and finds the shortest path across a planet-spanning network — without any single machine ever holding a map of the full route.

card.copy: How the Internet Routes Without a Map

beats.narration_text: Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths, but requires one machine with the complete graph. The internet has millions of routers. No machine can store the whole map. How does routing still find short paths?

production_viz.label: Global network with millions of nodes; one machine cannot hold full map; CRIMSON impossibility

beats.narration_text: Each router keeps one number per destination: its current best distance estimate. No route table, no global knowledge. Just one number per target, updated whenever neighbors report better distances.

card.copy: How do routers find shortest paths without a global map?
card.sub: One distance estimate per destination, updated from neighbors

beats.narration_text: The update rule: d_v gets the minimum over all neighbors w of link cost c_{vw} plus d_w. If any neighbor w says "I can reach the destination in d_w hops, and the link from v to w costs c_{vw}," then v's new estimate is at most c_{vw} plus d_w.

production_viz.label: Router v with neighbors; update rule d_v = min(c_vw + d_w); arrow from neighbor to v; TEAL formula

beats.narration_text: In round 1, only nodes adjacent to the destination have finite distance estimates. In round 2, their neighbors learn. In round 3, their neighbors' neighbors. The true shortest distances ripple outward from the destination in waves.

production_viz.label: Network with distance waves rippling outward from destination; rounds 1, 2, 3 colored; TEAL wave fronts

beats.narration_text: A 5-node chain S to A to B to C to T, all cost 1: T starts with d equals 0. Round 1: C learns d equals 1. Round 2: B learns d equals 2. Round 3: A learns d equals 3. Round 4: S learns d equals 4. Four rounds, correct, no central coordination.

production_viz.label: 5-node chain S-A-B-C-T; distance updates propagating round by round; TEAL arrows showing convergence

beats.narration_text: On the full internet, each round is a "routing update" — routers exchange distance vectors with their neighbors and update their estimates. After enough rounds, all estimates converge to true shortest distances. No router ever sees the whole graph.

production_viz.label: Distance vector exchange between routers; convergence after n-1 rounds; global shortest paths emerge locally; TEAL

beats.narration_text: This is the Bellman-Ford algorithm, published 1958. It underlies RIP — Routing Information Protocol — one of the earliest internet routing protocols. The modern internet uses more sophisticated variants, but the core idea — distributed distance updates — is the same.

production_viz.label: Bellman-Ford 1958 → RIP → modern internet; historical timeline; TEAL lineage

beats.narration_text: The key insight is that shortest-path computation is a fixed-point computation: the correct distance vector is the unique vector where every router's estimate is already the minimum over its neighbors' estimates plus link costs. Repeated updates converge to this fixed point.

production_viz.label: Fixed point diagram: d_v = min(c_vw + d_w) holds everywhere; convergence to unique solution; TEAL

beats.narration_text: Each router: one distance per destination, updated from neighbors. Rule: minimum of neighbor distance plus link cost. Converges in n-minus-1 rounds. No global map needed. The internet routes itself.

card.sub: Converges in n-1 rounds — no global map needed

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
